label: Chapter Nine
title: The Handwriting of Justice
question: What does trust become when oath, war, and beauty hold the same thread?
heroAlt: Jem, Marok, and Kitsu facing a dark mirror written in Kitsu's golden legal hand
summary: Kitsu's stolen oath-thread is reclaimed, Marok and Justice finally name the wound between them, and Jem refuses to be anyone's prize.
hook: Which half do they steal first: the Menagerie below, or the courthouse across the river?
body: The mirror wore Kitsu’s handwriting like a stolen face. Not ink. Not paint. Gold script burned across the dark glass in the exact legal hand I had watched him use in court: precise, merciless, so neat it made elegance look undisciplined.
NO FOXES. NO JUDGMENT.
The words rang without sound. Behind them, somewhere inside the mirror, half of the Scale sang in a low golden note that slipped beneath my ribs and worried the empty place behind my spine.

Kitsu did not move. Marok did. One step back. Only one. But in the Menagerie chamber, surrounded by shattered mirrors and smoke still green from his restored tail, that step was louder than accusation.
Kitsu heard it. Of course he did. His face remained calm, which meant nothing good had survived underneath. “That is my hand,” he said.
Marok’s restored tail flicked once behind him, bright green and furious. “Yes. We noticed.”
“I did not write it.”
“No one accused you.”
“You stepped away.”
Marok’s jaw tightened. “That was my body being sensible before my mouth could stop it.”
“An impressive novelty,” I said.
Neither of them smiled. Unkind.

The room held its breath around us. Black mirrors lay broken across the floor like a hundred dead witnesses. Every shard reflected the gold script differently. In one, the warning was backwards. In one, it was written across Kitsu’s throat. In another, his hand held the quill. I looked away from that one. Too late. The Menagerie had noticed me noticing.
The wall behind the mirror rippled. A woman’s laugh passed through the velvet, sweet as sugared rot. The hostess had not gone far.
“Justice signs beautifully,” she said from nowhere and everywhere. “Even when he does not remember doing it.”
Kitsu’s gold light snapped under his skin. “Show yourself,” he said.
“No,” the hostess replied. “I prefer being wanted.”
“No one wants you,” I said.
A pause. “Beauty lies prettily when cornered.”
I smiled at the mirror because my temper had found its posture. “Beauty bites when bored.”
The velvet walls shivered. Marok glanced at me as if he approved and worried in the same breath. An exhausting talent.

Kitsu stepped toward the mirror. Gold light gathered around his right hand, smelling of cold parchment and struck bells. The script answered. Each letter sharpened until the room filled with a sound like quills scraping bone.
“Do not touch it,” I said.
Kitsu stopped. Good. I disliked how quickly that pleased me.
“Why?” he asked.
I stepped over a shard of mirror. My borrowed feet objected. I ignored them. Feet had been overrepresented in my concerns since dawn and deserved less power.
The gold writing looked perfect. Naturally. Perfect things are where rot loves to hide.
I lifted my hand, stopped before my fingers reached the glass, and let Beauty sight open.

The room soured. Honey, smoke, and regret curdled into wet iron. The velvet darkened. The gold script softened at the edges, not melting, exactly. Bruising. Beneath the beautiful legal hand, something silver had been stitched through the letters.
Thread. No. Not thread. Oath-thread. Kitsu’s.
I lowered my hand. “Someone used you,” I said.
Kitsu went stiller than before. Marok’s face changed too. Less suspicion. More horror. That suited him badly.
“Explain,” Kitsu said.
I pointed at the writing. “The hand is yours. The will is not. Your oath-thread is laced beneath the words. Like a signature cut from skin and sewn onto a lie.”
Marok swore softly. Kitsu did not. That was worse.
“Impossible,” he said.
“Tonight has made that word cheap.”
His mouth tightened. “If my oath-thread was used, then something reached my tail.”
“Your tail is still missing,” Marok said.
“I am aware.”
“And now your hand is on the warning I received.”
“I am aware of that as well.”
“Were you aware before?”
The question landed between them. Hard. Ugly. Necessary.
Kitsu’s eyes cut to Marok. Gold, bright and dangerous. “No.”
“Truth?” Marok asked.
Kitsu’s expression became frighteningly polite. “Would you like me to bind myself and prove it?”
“I would like one answer tonight that does not arrive after everything breaks.”
“Then perhaps you should have given us the warning before the Scale did.”
Green fire flashed. Gold answered. The chamber warmed and chilled at once. I stepped between them before War and Justice could turn grief into architecture.
“Enough.”
They looked at me. Good.

My voice came sharper than I intended. “Marok hid a warning. Kitsu’s oath was stolen. I am missing a tail, wearing a coat that is not mine, and standing in a pleasure house where the furniture has opinions. If either of you needs a duel, schedule it after we survive.”
Marok looked away first. Kitsu did not. Of course.
“The writing is a lock,” I said. “And a lure. It wants Kitsu to touch it.”
“Because it is keyed to me,” Kitsu said.
“Because it wants whatever is left of you.”
His face did not move. “Useful distinction.”
“I thought so.”

Behind the mirror, the Scale half sang again. The note slipped through the floor and into every shard. The broken mirrors turned toward Kitsu like flowers seeking sunlight. In each one, he appeared different. Kitsu beside the God of Justice, gold tail immaculate. Kitsu alone in a hall of laws, both hands bloodless and clenched. Kitsu watching me dance with Marok. Kitsu kissing me in rainwater. Kitsu turning away too quickly afterward, as if desire were something he could apologize back into its cage.
My mouth went dry. Marok saw the reflections too. Of course he did. His restored tail lowered.
“Kitsu,” he said, quieter now.
Kitsu’s gaze remained fixed on the mirror. “Do not.”
“I was not—“
“Do not be kind because the room has become cruel.”
Marok’s face tightened. “That is not the only time I can manage it.”
Kitsu laughed once. No humor. Just a blade finding stone. “Forgive me. I had not noticed your excellent consistency.”

The words struck Marok. He absorbed them without the usual flourish. That made it hurt more. I hated both of them for making me care about the shape of their wounds while my own spine felt unfinished.
“Look at me,” I said.
Kitsu did. Stupid of him. Braver than he knew.

His control was there, but thin as ice over a river that had already decided to drown a city. The mirror had found his private want and dressed it in legal script, because of course a place like this knew the cruelest way to undress Justice was to make him appear guilty.
“I believe you,” I said.
The words surprised him. They surprised me more.
Marok looked at me, then at him. “So do I.”
Kitsu’s throat moved. “You stepped away,” he said.
“Yes,” Marok replied. “And then I came back.”
Truth. No gold wire needed.
The mirror cracked wider. Silver light leaked from the wound in the glass. The hostess laughed again, farther away now. Annoyed. Excellent.
“How do we open it?” Marok asked.
Kitsu looked at the gold script. “I must reclaim the oath-thread.”
“That sounds painful,” I said.
“Likely.”
“That was not concern. That was accusation.”
“Then accurately received.”
Marok stepped beside him. “What do you need?”
Kitsu glanced at him. Not distrust. Not forgiveness. Something unfinished.
“If I pull the thread free, the silver bind will try to write through me again.”
“Then I burn it,” Marok said.
“Only the silver.”
“I know.”
“Do you?”
Marok’s green eyes flashed. Then he breathed out. “Yes. I know.”
Kitsu looked at me. “And you reveal the lie.”
“Always.”
“No.” His voice softened. “Not always. Only if you choose.”
That was inconvenient. Worse, it was learned.
I stepped toward the mirror. “Then I choose.”
Kitsu lifted his hand toward the writing. Stopped. Looked at me. “May I use your sight as anchor?”
Marok went very still.

I understood the question beneath the question. Kitsu could not kiss me again. Not here. Not with Marok standing beside us still carrying the shape of my mouth on his own. Not with the Menagerie watching like a spider in velvet. But he needed an anchor. Want, restrained until it learned manners.
I held out my hand, palm inward. A choice he could step into or refuse. “You may hold my hand,” I said.
His fingers closed around mine. Careful. Too careful.
Then Marok’s hand appeared beside us, palm up, green fire hovering beneath his skin. “For the burn,” he said.
Kitsu looked at it. For one terrible heartbeat, I thought he would refuse. Then Justice took War’s hand.

The room changed. Rose sparked through my arm. Green flared in Marok’s palm. Gold tightened around Kitsu’s fingers. The braided thread snapped into being between us, brighter than before and painfully alive.
The mirror shrieked.
“Hold,” Kitsu said.
“I hate that word,” I said.
“I know.”
“Good.”
He touched the writing. Gold light exploded.
Kitsu’s body locked. His hand pressed flat to the mirror as the legal script raced up his wrist, over his forearm, toward his throat. Letters appeared beneath his skin.
NO FOXES.
NO JUDGMENT.

Marok swore and drove green fire into the silver thread. The room filled with the scent of rain striking hot stone, cold parchment, and extinguished candles. My sight opened so violently that every beautiful surface peeled back at once. The velvet became old rot. The mirrors became mouths. The gold writing became a hook lodged in Kitsu’s oath.
There. Rot beneath law.
“Now,” I gasped.

Marok burned. Not wildly. Not too much. He burned exactly what I showed him, green fire cutting the silver without touching gold. Kitsu shook between us. His grip tightened around my hand, then loosened at once. Always catching himself. Always caging himself before anyone else could.
“Do not disappear into it,” I said.
His eyes found mine through the light. “I am here.”
“Then stay.”

The gold script tore free of the mirror and snapped back into his palm. Kitsu made no sound. That was worse. His knees buckled. Marok caught him. Not by accident. Not as a joke. He stepped in and held him upright with one arm around his ribs, green fire still trembling in his other hand.
Kitsu stared at him. Marok stared back. “No debt,” Marok said, voice rough.
Kitsu’s expression broke for half a second. Not into softness. Into pain.

The mirror shattered outward without touching us. Behind it, half of the Scale of Judgment hung suspended in black air. One broken pan. Half a beam. Gold and blackened gold braided through with rose, green, and silver scars. It sang like a verdict underwater.
I reached for it. The Scale half withdrew. Rude.
A second note answered from far away. East. Higher. Colder. Law-scented and blood-bright. Kitsu’s head snapped toward it. “The other half,” he said.

The first half spun once in the air. In its broken surface, I saw two places at once. The Menagerie below us, roots of mirror and velvet wrapped around one half. A courthouse across the river, black gates and gold seals, where Kitsu’s missing tail burned like a captured sunrise.
Then, for one breath, my own magenta tail appeared behind both images. Not in a cage. In a crown.
I stepped back. Marok noticed. Kitsu noticed. Naturally.
“Two halves,” Marok said. “Two places.”
“A narrowing window,” Kitsu added.
“And only three of us,” I said.
The Scale half vanished into the dark behind the wall, still singing. Not gone. Waiting. The Menagerie exhaled around us. The danger did not pass. It merely became scheduled.
Marok released Kitsu as soon as Kitsu could stand. Kitsu straightened, perfect and pale and furious about needing anyone. Marok’s hand fell to his side as if it had never held him.

The lie between them remained. Not the warning. The other one. The one where Marok pretended not to care whether Kitsu trusted him. The one where Kitsu pretended distrust was safer than wanting anything from War. The one where both of them looked at me because it was easier than looking at each other.
I had tolerated many things tonight. This was not going on the list.
“Both of you,” I said.
They turned. “No.” I pointed between them. “At each other.”
Marok blinked. “Jem—“
“Do not Jem me in that tone. I have been dragged through mirrors, markets, false law, two kisses, and at least three emotional revelations I did not order. I am tired.”
Kitsu’s mouth tightened. “We do not have time for this.”
“That sentence is what cowards say when they are afraid honesty will take longer than survival.”
He went still. Good.
Marok looked at the broken mirror floor. “Kitsu thinks I caused this.”
“I think you contributed,” Kitsu said.
“There he is.”
“You did.”
“I know.”
The words were bare. Kitsu’s expression shifted.

Marok swallowed. “I know I broke the Scale. I know I hid the warning. I know I tried to be clever and made myself useful to a trap. I know.” His voice cracked. He hated that. I saw him hate it. “But I did not want you severed. Either of you.”
Kitsu looked at him for a long moment. Then he said, “I know.”
Marok laughed once. Broken. “That is all?”
“No,” Kitsu said. “It is all I can say without making it worse.”
“Try.”
The word surprised all of us. Especially Marok.
Kitsu’s hands curled at his sides. “When you are reckless, I become law because someone must survive the consequence.”
“And when you are law,” Marok said, “I become reckless because someone must prove there is a world outside your cage.”
Kitsu flinched. I did too. Not because it was cruel. Because it was accurate.
The room went quiet. I stepped toward them before either could retreat into dignity. “Excellent. Painful and useful. We are learning.”
“Are we?” Marok asked.
“Unfortunately.”
Kitsu looked at me then, and the heat in his eyes was not only from the stolen oath-thread. Marok looked too, green restored and raw. Want moved through the room like something with a pulse.
Desire was a door. Desire was also an argument no one had the courage to finish. I was done being the doorway they stared through.
I took Marok’s hand first. His breath caught. I took Kitsu’s next. His fingers trembled once before closing around mine.
“You both want me,” I said.
Marok’s eyes darkened. Kitsu stopped breathing.
“Do not insult me by pretending otherwise. Do not insult each other by using me as the place you hide from what that means.”
“Jem,” Kitsu said, barely sound.
“Ask,” I said.
Two gazes. Two hungers. Two kinds of fear.
Marok asked first. Of course. “May I?”
Kitsu’s voice followed, rougher than rain. “May I?”

I pulled them both down to me and kissed them before either could become noble about it. Marok tasted of smoke and honey and grief refusing to bow. Kitsu tasted of cold bells and restraint breaking under its own holiness. For one breath, they were not touching each other, only me between them. Then the braided thread flared rose-green-gold so bright the shattered mirrors lit like dawn.
Marok’s hand tightened at my waist and stopped there, waiting. Kitsu’s fingers touched my jaw and did not claim it. I broke the kiss before it could become a choice I had not made carefully enough.
My heart behaved terribly. My mouth worse. Both males looked ruined. Good.
“Figure out who gets me,” I said.
Marok made a sound like prayer and panic. Kitsu’s gold eyes went very still. I released them both.

“And by gets,” I added, because men and gods are poor listeners when desire is standing nearby, “I mean earns the right to be asked again. I am not a relic. I am not a reward. I am not the prettiest excuse either of you has for avoiding the other.”
Marok’s restored tail burned brighter. Kitsu’s stolen oath-thread glowed in his palm, clean now, his own again.
Somewhere behind the walls, one Scale half sang below us. Across the river, the other answered from the courthouse where Justice kept its cages. The Menagerie waited. The Bell breathed.
I wiped my mouth with the back of my hand and smiled because Beauty had taught me many things, including how to make danger look invited. “Now,” I said. “Which half do we steal first?”

scenes.alt: Kitsu's golden handwriting burns across a dark Menagerie mirror while Jem and Marok look on
scenes.caption: The mirror wore Kitsu's handwriting like a stolen face.

scenes.alt: Jem opens Beauty sight and reveals a silver oath-thread beneath Kitsu's golden script
scenes.caption: The hand is yours. The will is not.

scenes.alt: Jem, Marok, and Kitsu join hands before the cracked mirror as rose, green, and gold magic braid together
scenes.caption: Justice took War’s hand. The room changed.

scenes.alt: Marok burns the silver bind while Jem anchors Kitsu and gold legal script crawls over the mirror
scenes.caption: Marok burned exactly what Jem showed him.

scenes.alt: Marok catches Kitsu as the reclaimed oath-thread tears free from the mirror
scenes.caption: Marok caught him. Not by accident. Not as a joke.

scenes.alt: A broken Scale half reveals the Menagerie below and the courthouse across the river
scenes.caption: Two halves. Two places.

scenes.alt: Jem kisses both Marok and Kitsu as rose, green, and gold threads braid around them
scenes.caption: May I? May I?

scenes.alt: Jem between Marok and Kitsu before the river and courthouse as broken glass and Scale-light hover around them
scenes.caption: Now. Which half do we steal first?

label: Chapter Ten
title: Two Halves, One Choice
question: Which hand does Jem take when the Scale forces the night to divide?
heroAlt: Jem standing between Marok and Kitsu as both offer a hand and mirror paths open behind them
summary: The chosen path recovers one Scale half, the other fox returns with the second, and all three realize the next door is beneath Beauty itself.
hook: Below the city, something hidden sings back.
body: The Menagerie waited for my question to become a mistake.
Which half do we steal first? I had said it with all the confidence of a woman whose borrowed knees had not recently considered retirement. The room answered by singing.

Not loudly. That would have been merciful. The half of the Scale hidden behind the velvet wall released one low golden note, then let its twin answer from far across the river. Two sounds. Two graves. Two pieces of divine judgment pretending they had not once been whole.
Kitsu turned toward the distant note. Marok turned toward the near one. I remained between them, which was apparently my new spiritual vocation.

The shattered mirrors around us lifted their broken faces. Each shard reflected a different exit. One led down, under the Menagerie, into a dark root of velvet and want. The other showed rain, black gates, and a courthouse crouched across the river like a verdict waiting to be fed.
Inside the courthouse reflection, gold light burned behind iron bars. Kitsu’s tail. His face did not change. That was how I knew it hurt.
Marok’s restored green tail flared once behind him. Not with triumph. With anger. It suited him better than guilt, which had made him look too human and not nearly annoying enough.
The first Scale half sang beneath our feet again. The Menagerie answered with a shiver of velvet walls. Somewhere in the house, the hostess laughed softly, like a knife being wrapped for gift-giving.
“We are not alone,” Kitsu said.
“We have not been alone since the mirror learned to smile,” I replied.
Marok looked toward the broken wall. “The Menagerie half is close. Below us, I think. Desire has roots here. The house will have wrapped itself around the Scale like a drunk around a secret.”
Kitsu’s gaze remained on the courthouse reflection. “The other half has entered legal ground. If the next Bell rings before it is claimed, it may become part of the false commencement record.”
Naturally, the night had developed paperwork.
“What does that mean?” I asked.
“It means if we choose one together, we may lose the other,” Kitsu said.
Marok went still. “We split.”
“No,” Kitsu and I said together.
For a moment, that almost pleased me. Almost. Then the Scale sang again, and the sound crawled into the unfinished place behind my spine.
Kitsu closed his recovered oath-thread inside his fist. Gold light leaked between his fingers. “He is right.”
I hated the sentence for many reasons, beginning with he and ending with right.
Marok looked at me. Not grinning. Not performing. Worse. Waiting. The kind of waiting that offered me a door and did not push.

The mirror-shards rearranged themselves. Down, the Menagerie. Across, the courthouse. Below, want. Across, law. One route wrapped in velvet. One written in gold. Both shaped like cages that had learned my name.
Of course. The universe had finally found a way to make the choice insulting.
Kitsu spoke first. “Marok knows the Menagerie’s lower paths. I know the courthouse procedures.”
“Procedure is a very generous word for a building that appears to eat fox tails,” Marok said.
“You are welcome to bring your expertise in being eaten by pleasure houses.”
“I survived.”
“Barely.”
“Beautifully.”
I lifted one hand. “If you both flirt through jurisdictional crisis, I will choose the Scale and leave you here.”
Marok blinked. “Was that flirting?”
Kitsu’s mouth tightened. “No.”
Truth, but not enough of it.
The Menagerie trembled. The courthouse reflection brightened. The two Scale notes began to drift apart, one lower, one higher, as if the broken instrument were teaching us how to be divided.
Marok offered his hand, palm up. A choice, not a claim. “Come below with me. I know how this house lies.”
Kitsu offered his hand differently, palm inward. A threshold I could step into or refuse. “Come across with me. I know how the law lies.”
I stared at both hands.
Desire was a door. So was trust. So was stupidity, apparently, and all three had begun wearing attractive faces.
The next Bell breathed above the city. The glass would remember which hand I took.

Of course. The universe had finally found a way to make the choice insulting.
Kitsu spoke first. “Marok knows the Menagerie’s lower paths. I know the courthouse procedures.”
“Procedure is a very generous word for a building that appears to eat fox tails,” Marok said.

Kitsu offered his hand differently, palm inward. A threshold I could step into or refuse. “Come across with me. I know how the law lies.”
I stared at both hands.
Desire was a door. So was trust. So was stupidity, apparently, and all three had begun wearing attractive faces.
The next Bell breathed above the city. The glass would remember which hand I took.
— CHOICE · GO WITH MAROK —
If I had learned anything tonight, it was that going down rarely improved a situation. Naturally, I took Marok’s hand.

Kitsu’s fingers curled once, then fell to his side. He did not look away. That made leaving with Marok feel less like a victory and more like tearing a page from a book before knowing whether it held the answer.
“Do not die,” I told him.
Kitsu’s eyes moved from my hand in Marok’s to my face. “I had no plans.”
“Make better ones.”
Something almost warm crossed his face. Then he turned toward the courthouse reflection and stepped through it like a man entering his own execution with excellent posture. The mirror swallowed him.
Marok’s hand tightened around mine for exactly one heartbeat, then loosened. “No debt.”
“You are overusing that phrase.”
“I am trying to make it true.”
That was unfair. I followed him through the broken wall before I could say something kind by accident.

Below the Menagerie, velvet became root. The staircase curved downward through red-dark fabric that pulsed softly against the walls. Mirrors hung between folds like wet eyes. In each, I saw some version of want. Mortals kneeling. Spirits bargaining. My goddess smiling. Me standing alone with my magenta tail restored, all eyes on me and no hands at my throat.
Beautiful lie. I kept walking.
Marok’s shoulder brushed mine in the narrowing passage. “Too close?”
“You are large enough to be a structural problem.”
“That was not an answer.”
I looked at him. Even in green shadow, he waited. No grin. No assumption. Only heat trying to behave.
“Not too close,” I said.
His breath changed. Just once. The passage noticed and tightened around us like a gossip leaning in.

The Scale half lay in a chamber of black velvet roots, suspended over a shallow pool of mirror-water. It was one broken pan and half a beam, blackened gold wrapped in silver thread. The pool showed my tail in its cage. Magenta. Curled tight. Waiting.
My body moved toward it before my dignity could object.
Marok caught my sleeve. Not my wrist. The sleeve. He was learning so aggressively it was becoming inconvenient.
“Jem.”
“I see it.”
“It wants you to.”
Yes. It did. The pool showed my tail closer now. Not frightened. Not trapped. Offered. The beautiful kind of bait. The cruelest kind.

I forced my sight past it. Rot beneath ornament. The tail was not mine. Not here. Only a reflection of wanting stitched to the Scale half with silver. Veyr had built a door out of my desire and trusted me to mistake it for an exit.
Annoying. Accurate. Unforgivable.
“The thread is under the reflection,” I said.
Marok stepped beside me. Green fire gathered in his palms. “Tell me where to burn.”
“If I say stop—“
“I stop.”
“If you start dying impressively—“
“You will be cross.”
“I will be furious.”
His smile flickered. “Better.”
I placed my hand over the mirror-water. It was cold as moonlit glass. The silver thread surfaced, thin and bright, wrapped around the Scale half like a promise that had never intended to be kept.
“There,” I said.

War burned. Not wildly. Not for me. Not for applause. Marok’s green fire cut where I showed him and nowhere else. The thread shrieked. The pool filled with images of every bad decision he had ever made and three he had only considered. I admired the variety despite myself.
Then the reflection of my tail opened its eyes. Veyr’s eyes. Pale. Amused.
No foxes, their mouth shaped without sound.
“No,” I said.
Rose-magenta light tore from my hand. The beautiful lie peeled back. Marok’s fire caught the exposed silver and snapped it clean.
The Scale half dropped. Marok caught it with both hands and immediately went pale.
“That seems bad,” he said.
The broken pan burned green against his palms. Not consuming. Measuring. War holding judgment. A terrible idea, which meant it suited the evening perfectly.
I put my hands over his. “May I?”
He looked at me as if the word still surprised him. “Yes.”
Rose light wrapped over green. The burn eased. The Scale half quieted until it hummed instead of screamed.
For one breath, our hands were everywhere and nowhere: his beneath mine, mine steadying his, the heat between us trying very hard to become a confession.
We had the half.
Somewhere across the river, Kitsu’s note cut through the rain, gold and sharp with danger.
Marok and I looked up together.
“He found trouble,” Marok said.
“Of course he did.”
The mirror-water behind us turned black. In it, the courthouse gates opened like a mouth.
— PATH · SHARED RETURN —
However the glass remembered it, the city returned us to the same bridge.
One Scale half came back marked with velvet and green fire. The other came back marked with law and gold grief. The pieces sang toward each other across the wet air, not whole yet. Not even close.

Marok was bleeding again. Kitsu was paler than marble. I was wearing War’s coat, carrying Justice’s warmth in my bones, and missing the tail Veyr had shown me in a cage because apparently the night believed restraint built character.
It built rage, mostly.

The two Scale halves hovered between us. Rose light sparked along their broken edges. Green fire answered. Gold followed. For one breath, the braid wrapped around the pieces and pulled them almost together.
Almost.
Then the halves jerked apart.
A silver mark appeared along both broken seams: a fox tail curled into a question mark. Veyr’s invitation.
Kitsu looked toward the courthouse. Marok looked back at the Menagerie. I looked downriver, where the city’s glamour shimmered over grief I could suddenly smell beneath the rain.
Rot under ornament. A way in.
The next path was not to a half of the Scale. It was to what Beauty hid beneath the city.
I closed my hands around nothing and felt my missing tail answer from far away.
No foxes, Veyr had said. No Judgment.

I smiled because I had been made by Beauty, trained by a cage, and kissed by two disasters with excellent cheekbones. If Veyr thought I would reach for the prettiest trap first, he had not been paying attention.
“We are not chasing the tail,” I said.
Marok blinked. Kitsu went still.
“Not yet,” I added.
The Scale halves sang. Below the city, something hidden sang back.
kitsu: The Menagerie waited for my question to become a mistake.
Which half do we steal first? I had said it with all the confidence of a woman whose borrowed knees had not recently considered retirement. The room answered by singing.

Not loudly. That would have been merciful. The half of the Scale hidden behind the velvet wall released one low golden note, then let its twin answer from far across the river. Two sounds. Two graves. Two pieces of divine judgment pretending they had not once been whole.
Kitsu turned toward the distant note. Marok turned toward the near one. I remained between them, which was apparently my new spiritual vocation.

The shattered mirrors around us lifted their broken faces. Each shard reflected a different exit. One led down, under the Menagerie, into a dark root of velvet and want. The other showed rain, black gates, and a courthouse crouched across the river like a verdict waiting to be fed.
Inside the courthouse reflection, gold light burned behind iron bars. Kitsu’s tail. His face did not change. That was how I knew it hurt.
Marok’s restored green tail flared once behind him. Not with triumph. With anger. It suited him better than guilt, which had made him look too human and not nearly annoying enough.
The first Scale half sang beneath our feet again. The Menagerie answered with a shiver of velvet walls. Somewhere in the house, the hostess laughed softly, like a knife being wrapped for gift-giving.
“We are not alone,” Kitsu said.
“We have not been alone since the mirror learned to smile,” I replied.
Marok looked toward the broken wall. “The Menagerie half is close. Below us, I think. Desire has roots here. The house will have wrapped itself around the Scale like a drunk around a secret.”
Kitsu’s gaze remained on the courthouse reflection. “The other half has entered legal ground. If the next Bell rings before it is claimed, it may become part of the false commencement record.”
Naturally, the night had developed paperwork.
“What does that mean?” I asked.
“It means if we choose one together, we may lose the other,” Kitsu said.
Marok went still. “We split.”
“No,” Kitsu and I said together.
For a moment, that almost pleased me. Almost. Then the Scale sang again, and the sound crawled into the unfinished place behind my spine.
Kitsu closed his recovered oath-thread inside his fist. Gold light leaked between his fingers. “He is right.”
I hated the sentence for many reasons, beginning with he and ending with right.
Marok looked at me. Not grinning. Not performing. Worse. Waiting. The kind of waiting that offered me a door and did not push.

The mirror-shards rearranged themselves. Down, the Menagerie. Across, the courthouse. Below, want. Across, law. One route wrapped in velvet. One written in gold. Both shaped like cages that had learned my name.
Of course. The universe had finally found a way to make the choice insulting.
Kitsu spoke first. “Marok knows the Menagerie’s lower paths. I know the courthouse procedures.”
“Procedure is a very generous word for a building that appears to eat fox tails,” Marok said.
“You are welcome to bring your expertise in being eaten by pleasure houses.”
“I survived.”
“Barely.”
“Beautifully.”
I lifted one hand. “If you both flirt through jurisdictional crisis, I will choose the Scale and leave you here.”
Marok blinked. “Was that flirting?”
Kitsu’s mouth tightened. “No.”
Truth, but not enough of it.
The Menagerie trembled. The courthouse reflection brightened. The two Scale notes began to drift apart, one lower, one higher, as if the broken instrument were teaching us how to be divided.
Marok offered his hand, palm up. A choice, not a claim. “Come below with me. I know how this house lies.”
Kitsu offered his hand differently, palm inward. A threshold I could step into or refuse. “Come across with me. I know how the law lies.”
I stared at both hands.
Desire was a door. So was trust. So was stupidity, apparently, and all three had begun wearing attractive faces.
The next Bell breathed above the city. The glass would remember which hand I took.
— CHOICE · GO WITH KITSU —
I took Kitsu’s hand.
Marok’s face did not fall. That would have been too easy to forgive. Instead, his smile appeared for half a second and behaved itself badly.
“Do not let the courthouse flirt with you,” he said.
“I will file a complaint if it tries.”
His restored tail flicked once. Green, bright, too alive for how tired he looked. “No debt,” he said.
Kitsu looked at him. “Do not die.”
Marok’s grin softened into something almost serious. “Try to sound less heartbroken about it.”
“Marok.”
“I know. Wrong moment.”
Then he turned toward the lower Menagerie passage and vanished into velvet before either of us could make the moment worse by improving it.
The courthouse reflection swallowed Kitsu and me whole.
Rain struck first. Then cold. Then law.

We stood before black gates across the river, the courthouse rising beyond them in layers of gold seals and dark glass. Every window reflected Kitsu. Not me. Not the street. Kitsu beside the God of Justice. Kitsu without his tail. Kitsu with his hand on a document I could not read. Kitsu kneeling in a room where every law had teeth.
He did not look at any of them. Naturally, I did.
“Your building is rude,” I said.
“It is not my building.”
“It is looking at you like you left it at an altar.”
A muscle moved in his jaw. “Do not bargain with anything beyond the gates.”
“Do I look like someone inclined to bargain with architecture?”
“You look like someone inclined to insult it until it opens.”
The gates unlocked.
I smiled. Kitsu did not, but his silence developed texture.

Inside, the courthouse smelled of old rain, cold parchment, struck bells, and something uglier underneath. Spoiled roses. Beauty magic dressed in legal robes. Of course Veyr had learned to accessorize.
Gold script floated above the central hall.
NO FOXES. NO JUDGMENT.
This time the words were not in Kitsu’s hand. They were trying to become it.
His fingers tightened around mine. Not hard. Enough that I knew he had forgotten he was touching me. Enough that, a heartbeat later, he remembered and loosened at once.
“Stay,” I said.
He looked at me.
“I did not tell you to let go.”
His face became unreadable in the way a locked chest is unreadable while containing a live coal. “Understood.”
We crossed the hall together.

At the center, half of the Scale hung above a judge’s bench carved from black glass. Beside it, behind a lattice of gold procedure, Kitsu’s tail burned like captured sunrise. Straight. Beautiful. Furious. It kept trying to reach him and being corrected by the law around it.
Kitsu stopped breathing.
I hated that tail for hurting him by existing just out of reach.
“The Scale first,” he said.
“That was not what your face said.”
“My face is not the authority.”
“Tragic. It makes several convincing arguments.”
He ignored that with what I suspected was effort.
The gold lattice shifted. Three seals appeared: CLAIM, CONFESS, WITNESS.
“Procedure,” Kitsu said, because apparently the courthouse wished to seduce him specifically.
“Trap,” I said, because I had eyes.
“Both.”
At least we agreed.

I opened Beauty sight. The seals changed. CLAIM glittered brightest, a lovely thing with rot around the edges. CONFESS bled silver. WITNESS looked plain. Almost dull. Which meant it was probably the only honest door in the room.
“Witness,” I said.
Kitsu glanced at me. “The weakest seal.”
“The least pretty one.”
“That is not legal reasoning.”
“It is better.”
For one impossible second, he almost smiled.
He placed his recovered oath-thread against WITNESS. The seal opened. Silver wire lashed out from CLAIM and wrapped around his wrist.
Kitsu did not cry out. Of course he did not. His restraint remained committed to being irritating.
I grabbed the wire with both hands. It burned cold. Beneath it, I saw the lie: Veyr did not need Kitsu to sign the false commencement. He needed Kitsu to believe he already had.
“It is guilt,” I said. “Not law.”
Kitsu’s eyes snapped to mine.
“You think if you had reported every suspicion, obeyed every procedure, anticipated every rot beneath every beautiful thing, none of this would have happened.”
The wire tightened.
“Jem.”
“No. Hear it. This is not your signature. This is your punishment dressed as duty.”
Gold light flared so bright my teeth hurt.
Kitsu lifted his free hand. Stopped. Looked at me. “May I use your sight?”
“You are already holding my hand, Golden One.”
“May I lean on it?”
That was much worse.
“Yes,” I said.
He did. Not with his body. With his trust. Gold law passed through my sight, clean and terrible. I revealed the silver guilt-thread. He cut it with one precise motion.
The lattice opened. The Scale half dropped into his palm.
His tail surged toward him. Kitsu took one step. The courthouse screamed.
The gold lattice slammed down between him and the tail. Every window darkened. False procedure sealed around the tail again, tighter than before.
“No,” I said.
Kitsu’s hand closed around the Scale half until gold light spilled between his fingers. His expression remained calm. Ruined, but calm.
“Not yet,” he said.
“Kitsu.”
“If I break the lattice now, the false commencement takes jurisdiction. We need the Court House proper.”
That sounded like law. It smelled like grief.
I wanted to argue. I wanted to tear the lattice apart with my teeth. Instead, I stepped beside him and looked at his tail burning behind the bars.
“We come back,” I said.
He did not look at me. “Yes.”
“Not as procedure.”
His throat moved.
“As promise,” I said.
He looked then.
The Scale half hummed between us, gold and blackened gold, warming to my rose light and his recovered oath. Across the river, green fire answered from somewhere beneath the Menagerie.
Marok had found his half.
Kitsu and I stood in a courthouse full of beautiful law and left his tail behind.
Not surrendered. Not forgotten. Witnessed.
The gates opened behind us. Rain waited like absolution with poor manners.
— PATH · SHARED RETURN —
However the glass remembered it, the city returned us to the same bridge.
One Scale half came back marked with velvet and green fire. The other came back marked with law and gold grief. The pieces sang toward each other across the wet air, not whole yet. Not even close.

Marok was bleeding again. Kitsu was paler than marble. I was wearing War’s coat, carrying Justice’s warmth in my bones, and missing the tail Veyr had shown me in a cage because apparently the night believed restraint built character.
It built rage, mostly.

The two Scale halves hovered between us. Rose light sparked along their broken edges. Green fire answered. Gold followed. For one breath, the braid wrapped around the pieces and pulled them almost together.
Almost.
Then the halves jerked apart.
A silver mark appeared along both broken seams: a fox tail curled into a question mark. Veyr’s invitation.
Kitsu looked toward the courthouse. Marok looked back at the Menagerie. I looked downriver, where the city’s glamour shimmered over grief I could suddenly smell beneath the rain.
Rot under ornament. A way in.
The next path was not to a half of the Scale. It was to what Beauty hid beneath the city.
I closed my hands around nothing and felt my missing tail answer from far away.
No foxes, Veyr had said. No Judgment.

I smiled because I had been made by Beauty, trained by a cage, and kissed by two disasters with excellent cheekbones. If Veyr thought I would reach for the prettiest trap first, he had not been paying attention.
“We are not chasing the tail,” I said.
Marok blinked. Kitsu went still.
“Not yet,” I added.
The Scale halves sang. Below the city, something hidden sang back.

Desire was a door. So was trust. So was stupidity, apparently, and all three had begun wearing attractive faces.
The next Bell breathed above the city. The glass would remember which hand I took.
— CHOICE · CHOOSE BOTH —
I looked at Marok’s hand. Then Kitsu’s. Then the space between them, which the night kept pretending was empty.
It was not empty. It was full of every thing they had not said, every wound they had dressed as doctrine, every want Beauty had taught me to fear because wanting made a person visible.
So I did the insulting thing. The honest thing. I took both hands.
Marok inhaled. Kitsu went still. The Scale made a sound so offended it nearly became language.
“That is not one route,” Kitsu said.
“No,” I replied. “It is mine.”
Marok’s thumb moved once against my knuckles and stopped there, waiting. “Jem.”
“No one is being chosen as a consolation prize,” I said. “No one is being kept as a secret second answer. If the night wants us divided, it can learn the difference between distance and surrender.”

Rose light slipped from my hand into both of theirs. Green answered first, reckless and warm. Gold followed, clean and terrified of wanting anything it could not justify. The three threads braided once, not enough to make the paths one road, but enough to make the split remember us.
The Menagerie opened below Marok like a dark red throat. The courthouse brightened behind Kitsu, all black gates and gold grief. They looked at each other across me.
“Do not die,” Kitsu said.
Marok’s smile was small, tired, and almost careful. “You either.”
I squeezed both hands once. Permission. Promise. Warning.
Then the city divided us without managing to make me choose only one heart to follow.
— PATH · SHARED RETURN —

scenes.alt: Jem between Marok and Kitsu as the Menagerie and courthouse paths split in shattered mirrors
scenes.caption: One route wrapped in velvet. One written in gold.

marok.alt: Jem holds Marok’s hand while Kitsu walks alone into the courthouse mirror
marok.caption: Leaving with Marok felt like tearing a page from a book.

marok.alt: Jem and Marok descend a staircase of red-black velvet roots and desire mirrors
marok.caption: Below the Menagerie, velvet became root.

marok.alt: Jem reaches toward mirror-water showing her caged magenta tail while Marok catches her sleeve
marok.caption: The beautiful kind of bait. The cruelest kind.

marok.alt: Jem, Marok, and Kitsu return to the rain-slick bridge with both Scale halves hovering between them
marok.caption: The pieces sang toward each other across the wet air.

kitsu.alt: Jem and Kitsu hold hands before black courthouse gates in the rain while Kitsu’s reflected history surrounds them
kitsu.caption: Rain struck first. Then cold. Then law.

kitsu.alt: Inside the courthouse, Jem and Kitsu face three symbolic seals while Kitsu’s golden tail burns behind a lattice
kitsu.caption: Claim. Confess. Witness.

kitsu.alt: Kitsu holds a broken Scale half while his golden tail remains trapped behind courthouse bars and Jem stands beside him
kitsu.caption: Not surrendered. Not forgotten. Witnessed.

kitsu.alt: Jem, Marok, and Kitsu return to the rain-slick bridge with both Scale halves hovering between them
kitsu.caption: The pieces sang toward each other across the wet air.

both.alt: Jem stands between Marok and Kitsu as the Menagerie and courthouse paths divide around all three of them
both.caption: The glass remembered both hands.

both.alt: Jem, Marok, and Kitsu return to the rain-slick bridge with both Scale halves hovering between them
both.caption: Distance was not surrender. The pieces still sang back.
